sentences.text: My name is Max Grosblat.
Last name is G-R-O-S-B-L-A-T.
Dubno, Poland.
Dubno is D-U-B-N-O.
I was born in 1922, March 7, 1922.
Today, I'm 73 years old.
My family was strictly orthodox.
We belonged to a shul that was a Hasidic shul.
Hasidic shul-- even it was called Hasidic shul.
I can't remember the rabbi.
We had living quarters plus a working shop where my dad used to work, in the same house.
He was a tailor.
Mom was our housekeeper.
Well, it was a nice life, quiet and happy.
Yes, I had four brothers and one sister.
Yes, my sister was Pearl.
She was born 1926, 1924, two years after I was born.
I was the oldest.
Then came Pearl, and then came Shlomo.
And Shlomo was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the third.
After Shlomo, then came Benjamin.
Yeah, he was the youngest.
Well, when it used to rain outside, we used to run out in the rain and play outside.
We loved to play in the rain.
Well, of course, Mother didn't approve of it, because she had to change our clothes.
That was a lot of fun.
Most important activity that I remember for me was swimming.
We-- we lived close to a river.
And I used to go down there every day and swim.
About seven years old.
No, not as big as the Mississippi.
It was quite a small river.
Yes, sometimes the whole family went.
Yes, I went.
I went to cheder and then I went to yeshiva.
My bar mitzvah wasn't as elaborate as they make it right now.
I was called to the Torah.
And I recited a-- the portion of the Torah that I was supposed to recite.
And--
And then we had some refreshments.
And there was no fancy dinner, such things like that.
The kids in our neighborhood formed a group, we played soccer.
Yes, [NON-ENGLISH], number two.
[NON-ENGLISH] means not quite a full street, just like a little, small street-- [NON-ENGLISH].
It was mostly, probably all, Jewish.
Yes, we were all Jewish there.
Also, across the street from my home, there was a Polish lady and her husband, Larry.
Yes, I remember them very well.
Because when I came back after the war, I stayed there for-- for a little while, until I went to emigrate to Germany.
I stayed in their house.
No, it's been a long time.
And I can't remember.
Yes, it was.
Well, right after 1939, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Russians came in.
And they occupied our area.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in 1941, the Germans came in.
And they occupied our area.
June, I think, 19-- 1939.
I think 1941, also June.
No, thank you.
Well, people were actually very sad and crying, because when the Russians came in, they did not allow any free enterprise.
And most of the people that lived there were small grocery man, small shop man.
They were afraid to lose all that.
Well, the economy in Dubno was a good economy for the Jews.
A very good economy, because Dubno was in a very rich agricultural area.
We had fruit, and poultry, and eggs that we shipped abroad.
The Jews were go between the farmer and the buyers in the other country.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were packing the eggs, and sending them out, packing the poultry, and packing fruit.
And they sent them all over the world.
No.
Yes, my family came from Germany in the 1880s.
When the Crusaders came out to go to-- to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] free-- free the Holy Land from the infidels, they-- they started on the path of killing the Jews in their path.
So my family ran away from Germany.
We settled in Poland.
No, they never went back.
We settled in Poland.
A very sad situation.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] we heard a lot of bad stories of what was going on in-- in Germany and in Russia.
And we were all fearful that that same thing is going to happen in our area.
Yeah, in the beginning.
They said they were taking people.
And people disappeared.
Nobody knew where they where.
They just disappeared.
So they wait it out and see what happen.
When the Russians came in, they closed the cheder.
They closed the yeshiva.
It was interrupted.
There was not only doing the war, it was even before the war was a lot of anti-Semitism.
Well, I remember when it was about 5 o'clock on Sundays.
We had to stay away from the churches, because when they came out of the service, they start hitting any Jews they saw.
So we have to stay away from the-- from the area of-- of churches, yes.
I think the worst was around Christmas time.
Because the Jews were accused of killing Christ.
Actually, we had two wars there.
The first war was Russia invaded our area.
And the second war was where-- when Germany invaded our area.
Mm-hmm.
To us personally, they didn't do very much.
But to the people that had small-- small businesses, they-- locked them up-- they couldn't have any more businesses.
They locked them up.
They locked the business up.
People had to make a living best way they knew how.
My father was still working as a tailor.
And he was still in a synagogue singing during the holidays.
It was harder and harder to make a living.
So I took the responsibility.
I said I can somehow make some money.
So that-- And I used to go from our town to Lemberg.
Lemberg, it's Lviv.
We-- over there used to be a lot of products that you couldn't get them in Dubno at that time.
I used to go by train and buy a lot of-- a lot of things that we couldn't get them in Dubno.
Then bring them and sell them on-- on the market.
That's where we had some income, and we could exist.
I bought it from the people that were selling it in Lemberg.
There's a market, open market.
So I went there.
And sometimes I bought some vodka, sometimes I bought some soap, sometimes I bought some other items that I know they're scarce in Dubno.
And when I brought them back, I sold them at a profit.
And I gave the money to my parents to have enough money to support us.
I went with some of my friends who were doing the same thing.
Oh, not too big, a backpack.
A backpack, we packed it so we could carry it with us all the time, because sometimes they were chasing us.
We had to run.
So we made backpacks.
The Russians.
Yeah, the Russians didn't give us too much trouble at that time, except for the people that were small merchants.
They locked them out of their business.
That was a terrific blow to the people, took away their income.
People had to sell their prized possessions in order to exist.
Yes, it did.
But we didn't have any business.
We-- my father was working as a tailor.
And that they couldn't take away from him, because the [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] population around Dubno was mostly Ukrainians, and Polish, and Germans, and Russians.
So when they wanted something, a new suit, a new coat, they had to come to my dad.
My dad should make it for them.
Mm-hmm.
Yes, I do.
The advanced German-- German militaries that came into our town, they were all on motorcycles.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We stood there and watched them, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] all on motorcycles.
No, we didn't have any warning.
So all of a sudden, we start seeing those German soldiers on motorcycles, coming into town.
And that's all.
Not quite a deep blue, it's light blueish.
Wehrmacht.
I think it was about 1 o'clock.
No, I can't remember the day.
But I know we all ran out there to watch them come in.
Then we had to live with it.
And they gave-- they gave orders to-- to where we lived.
They only gave us about [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] four blocks to live in, between east and west, and seven blocks between north and south.
That area was for-- a ghetto.
We had to build a 10-foot-- 10 feet fence around that area.
And we couldn't get out from there.
And they had to go through guards to come in and go out.
They posted guards around there.
No.
Yes, my aunt and her family.
Yes, very bad.
There was no food.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When you went out-- out from the ghetto on the street, you had to be careful not to walk on the sidewalk.
Because if you walked on the sidewalk, they'd just throw you off the sidewalk.
The German soldiers.
Yes.
We had to wear a star.
Yes, it did make me angry.
Worse, yes.
Well, we had to take our prized possessions and trade them for some food.
The farmers used to come to the-- to the ghetto, not inside the ghetto.
We used to trade them.
They were on the outside of the ghetto.
We were on the inside of the ghetto.
We used to trade our prized possessions for some food.
Like a gold watch and a gold ring.
That's right.
They-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they took the-- all able-bodied to work.
We had to go to a certain designated area.
And they came and they picked out 10 or 20 people.
And they took them to work.
Yes.
They were too young.
OK.
When the Germans first came, they had me working making roads, asphalt.
We had to make better roads for them that they could advance faster.
We had cobblestones and that wasn't good enough for them.
They had to have a smooth road so they can advance faster.
So they took us out.
And we worked on the roads until later on, when the Gendarmerie came.
Gendamerie was a police.
They were sent to take care of law and order in our town.
When they came in, they needed people to work for them.
So they sent me over to the Gendamerie there-- myself and my cousin, were-- we were using wood for cooking and for heating.
We had to sew it and cut it to a certain size, so it would fit in the ovens and the stoves.
So my cousin and I were sent to work for the Gendarmerie.
